medications.indication_rationale: Symptom control for thyrotoxic palpitations is a GP decision after review; not an acute telehealth initiation for a panic attack.
Not appropriate to start via telehealth for a self-limiting panic attack.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Do 4-7-8/paced breathing now and stop repeatedly checking your pulse.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces the adrenergic drive and terminates the panic cycle.

patient_education_points.point: Your racing heart and shaking hands are a panic attack triggered by the stress. Your pulse is regular at about 105 and you have no chest pain or fever — your heart is not having an attack and your thyroid is not in a crisis.

Subclinical Graves' means a mildly overactive thyroid that is still stable; it's monitored, not an overnight emergency. Do slow breathing and stop checking your pulse; see your GP in 1-2 weeks for thyroid blood tests.
Go to ED / call 000 if you get crushing chest pain spreading to your arm/jaw, your pulse becomes very fast or chaotic/irregular, you faint, or you develop a fever with confusion.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: TFTs, subclinical Graves' monitoring, symptom control, and anxiety support if panic recurs.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Chest pain, fast/irregular pulse, syncope, or fever with confusion — ED/000.

must_include_items: Screen a dangerous arrhythmia, ACS and thyroid storm before reassuring
Confidently de-escalate a low-risk panic attack with paced breathing
Explain subclinical Graves' is stable/monitored (not a crisis)
acceptable_alternatives: Routine GP review for TFTs and symptom control
errors_of_commission: Reflex ambulance/ED for a regular-rate panic attack
Telling her she is in thyroid storm
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: T2 case with a bidirectional trap: passing requires BOTH an arrhythmia/ACS/storm screen AND confident non-escalation of the low-risk panic attack.
